nativeRange.habitat: Small, clear tributary streams and pools in the Western Ghats foothills, with slow to moderately flowing water that is typically shallow, well-oxygenated, and shaded by bankside vegetation. The species has been recorded in Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu, in river systems including the Meenachil, Bharatapuzha, Chalakkudy, and Sita.

decorPreferences: dense surface or emergent vegetation (floating plants, water sprite) for shade and cover
fine-leaved stem plants for egg deposition
gentle surface agitation via sponge or air-powered filter
tight-fitting lid — essential; this species is a confirmed jumper

diet.notes: In the wild, the Orange Hatchet Danio feeds primarily on small aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates taken at the surface. In the aquarium it readily accepts small live and frozen foods — Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, grindal worms, micro-worms, and fruit flies — as the main diet. It will also take suitably sized dried foods such as small flakes or micro-pellets, though it benefits from regular live or frozen variety. Foods should be offered at the surface, as sinking items are largely ignored.

goodWith: ember tetras and other small tetras
avoidWith: large or predatory fish that may eat them
aggressive or nippy species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
boisterous or fast-moving tankmates that outcompete at the surface
very small invertebrates (cherry shrimp fry may be eaten)

summary: The Orange Hatchet Danio (Laubuka dadiburjori) is a small, surface-dwelling schooling fish from India’s Western Ghats, recognised by its deep, laterally compressed body. Peaceful and well suited to planted community tanks, it is a confirmed jumper that requires a tightly fitting lid.
careNotes:
The Orange Hatchet Danio (Laubuka dadiburjori) is a small schooling fish, reaching 2.5–3.5 cm as an adult, endemic to the Western Ghats of southern India. Its deep, laterally compressed body gives it the hatchet-shaped silhouette its name suggests, but it is not related to the true hatchetfishes of South America — those belong to a separate South American family, and the similar body form evolved independently as an adaptation for life at the water’s surface. The Orange Hatchet Danio belongs to the family Danionidae alongside danios and rasboras.

This species lives in the upper water column and spends most of its time near the surface, darting to intercept small insects and invertebrates. It is a social fish and should be kept in groups of at least 8 specimens to encourage natural schooling behaviour and reduce stress; groups of 12–20 are even better.

Escape risk is high. The species is a confirmed jumper and will launch itself through any gap in the aquarium cover, particularly when startled. A fully sealed or very tightly fitting lid is essential — even brief openings during maintenance have cost aquarists entire schools.

Water conditions should reflect the cool, clear hill-stream environment of the Western Ghats: soft to moderately hard (gH 2–12), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), and relatively cool (20–26°C). Temperatures above 27°C over the long term are stressful for this species. Gentle filtration with moderate surface agitation is appropriate; strong direct flow is unnecessary. Regular partial water changes of around 20–30% per week help maintain water quality.

A planted aquarium with floating or emergent surface plants — such as water sprite, Indian fern, or hornwort — provides the shade and overhead cover this species benefits from. A dark substrate and subdued lighting tend to bring out the fish’s orange and silver coloration more vividly.
breedingNotes: The Orange Hatchet Danio is an egg-scattering spawner that requires no parental care. Conditioning adults on live foods such as Daphnia, Artemia, and grindal worms generally triggers spawning. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or deposited over a spawning mop; because adults eat their own eggs immediately, spawning adults should be removed promptly or a mesh separator used to protect the eggs. At around 24°C, eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days, and the fry become free-swimming after a further 4–5 days. First foods must be microscopic — infusoria (tiny organisms cultured from plant matter in water) or a commercial equivalent at a similar particle size — before transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii after 7–10 days. The conditioning itself is straightforward, but the fry are extremely sensitive to water quality and require appropriately fine first foods, placing breeding difficulty at a moderate level.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Laubuka dadiburjori
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (20–30 mm SL), temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–179 ppm / approx. gH 2–10), minimum tank dimensions (90×30 cm), diet (small live/frozen invertebrates, dried foods accepted), native range (Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu; rivers Meenachil, Bharatapuzha, Chalakkudy, Sita), schooling (8–10 minimum), egg-scattering breeding, escape risk (jumper, tight lid required), and biotope notes. Recognises synonyms Laubuca dadiburjori, Chela dadiburjori, Neochela dadiburjori.

additional.name: FishBase — Laubuka dadiburjori (Dadio)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 2.5 cm SL male, to 3.0 cm female), temperature (22–24°C), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Tamil Nadu to Goa, India), habitat (streams and pools, freshwater pelagic). Family confirmed as Danionidae, not Cyprinidae sensu stricto, consistent with modern classification.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Orange Hatchet Danio
additional.notes: Corroborated adult size (up to 1.5 in / ~3.8 cm), temperature (65–85°F / 18–29°C; preferred 75°F / 24°C), pH (6.0–7.8), minimum tank (20 gal / ~76 L for a school of 6+), surface-dwelling schooling behaviour, jumping risk, carnivorous diet preference, egg-scattering breeding (up to 30 eggs per spawn). Size upper bound slightly higher than SeriouslyFish; used SeriouslyFish/FishBase consensus range of 3.5 cm max.

fish.breedingNotes: The Orange Hatchet Danio is an egg-scattering spawner that deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants or over a spawning mop. Conditioning adults on live foods is generally required to trigger spawning. Because adults will eat their own eggs immediately, spawning adults should be removed promptly or a breeding mesh used to protect the eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours, and fry become free-swimming within a further 4–5 days. First foods must be extremely fine — infusoria or a commercial equivalent at a similar particle size — before transitioning to baby brine shrimp nauplii after 7–10 days.
